state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Token Safety Screening Bot

Summary:
This bot helps Telegram users quickly screen crypto tokens against a practical set of safety-oriented heuristics: ownership renounced, liquidity locked or burned, and concentration checks such as top holders owning no more than 25% collectively. The product is not positioned as financial advice or a guarantee of legitimacy. Instead, it acts as a fast decision-support tool that aggregates token metadata, contract state, liquidity signals, and holder distribution into a clear, explainable Telegram response.

The production-ready version should focus on three outcomes: 1) reliable token lookup from user-submitted addresses or symbols, 2) deterministic evaluation rules with transparent pass/fail reasons, and 3) low-latency responses backed by caching and durable persistence where useful. Since this runs as a single Node.js service using grammY, the design should keep the runtime simple, avoid unnecessary infrastructure, and separate concerns cleanly across update handling, blockchain data adapters, scoring logic, and persistence.

Product goals:
The primary goal is to let a Telegram user submit a token identifier and receive a structured safety screen in seconds. The result should clearly answer whether the token appears to satisfy the requested conditions: ownership renounced, liquidity locked or burned, and top-10 holder concentration below the configured threshold. The bot should also show the evidence used for each result so users understand why a token passed or failed.

A second goal is to make the evaluation configurable without overwhelming users. The default profile can check a standard set of rules, while advanced users can optionally select a chain, adjust thresholds such as top-10 concentration, or request a more detailed report. This keeps the first-time experience simple while preserving flexibility for serious users.

A third goal is operational trustworthiness. Production readiness here means stable command handling, clear error reporting when a token cannot be resolved, protection against spam and repeated costly lookups, and a persistence model that stores user preferences, recent scans, and optional watchlists. The bot should be explicit about limitations: liquidity lock status may depend on supported locker sources, ownership renounce semantics vary by contract design, and holder concentration can change rapidly.

A fourth goal is explainability. Rather than returning a single opaque score, the bot should report a checklist with pass, fail, or unknown states for each rule, plus supporting details such as owner address status, liquidity pool location, lock/burn evidence, and top-holder share percentages. This makes the bot more useful and defensible than a simplistic yes/no label.

High-level architecture:
The system should be designed as one Node.js service composed of several logical modules. grammY handles Telegram updates, command routing, middleware, and conversational prompts where needed. A bot application layer translates user input into scan requests, validates chain and token identifiers, and orchestrates downstream services.

The core domain layer should contain a token screening engine. This engine receives a normalized scan request and applies a deterministic rule set. It should not know about Telegram-specific concerns. Its job is to gather facts from blockchain data adapters, transform them into a common internal model, evaluate each rule, and produce a structured verdict with evidence, timestamps, and confidence notes.

Below that, an integration layer should contain chain-specific and source-specific adapters. These adapters query supported blockchain RPC endpoints and any external metadata/indexing sources required for ownership status, pair discovery, liquidity positions, lock or burn evidence, and holder distribution. Because third-party data quality can vary, adapters should annotate source reliability and return partial results cleanly. The screening engine should be resilient to missing fields and mark a rule as unknown rather than failing the entire scan.

Persistence can be split into two levels. First, MongoDB should store durable records such as users, chat preferences, scan history, saved watchlists, supported chain settings, and cached scan results with freshness metadata. Second, in-memory maps can be used for short-lived rate-limit counters, in-flight request deduplication, and hot cache entries to avoid duplicate expensive lookups during traffic spikes. This keeps the single-process runtime fast without requiring additional infrastructure.

The update flow should be simple. A Telegram user sends a command, token address, or a message containing a contract address. grammY middleware normalizes the message, checks rate limits, identifies the chain if provided or infers it from address format/context, and submits a scan request. The screening engine either returns a fresh cached result or performs a live scan. The response formatter then builds a concise Telegram message and optionally a detailed follow-up.

To support maintainability, the architecture should define strict boundaries: 1) Telegram interface layer, 2) application orchestration layer, 3) domain screening engine, 4) data adapters, and 5) persistence/cache layer. This allows future extension to more chains, more safety rules, or alerting features without reworking the bot surface.

Data model:
The core durable entities should be small and practical.

1) User
Fields should include Telegram user ID, username snapshot, locale, created-at, updated-at, per-user settings, preferred chain, preferred thresholds, and whether the user wants concise or detailed output.

2) Chat
This entity is useful if the bot is added to groups. Fields should include Telegram chat ID, chat type, title snapshot, enabled features, admins cache if needed for permission checks, and group-level scan defaults. Group settings can differ from private user settings.

3) TokenScanRequest
This is an input/audit record containing requester ID, chat ID, token identifier as entered, normalized token address, chain, requested rule profile, requested thresholds, source command, and request timestamp. It helps trace what the user asked for.

4) TokenScanResult
This is the main persisted output. Fields should include token address, chain, token metadata snapshot, scan timestamp, source freshness timestamps, and an array of rule evaluations. Each rule evaluation should contain rule name, status as pass/fail/unknown, reason text, evidence references, and normalized metrics. Example metrics include owner address status, top-10 holder percentage, liquidity pool addresses, LP token supply distribution, lock expiration if known, and burn address balances if relevant.

5) TokenMetadataCache
This stores reusable token facts that change infrequently, such as symbol, name, decimals, total supply methodology, deployer address, known pool addresses, and supported DEX references. It should have a last-verified timestamp and a freshness policy.

6) WatchlistEntry
If watchlists are supported, each entry should include owner user or chat, token address, chain, thresholds, last status summary, and alert preferences. This enables periodic rescans within the same single service if lightweight scheduling is added later.

7) ChainConfig
This holds supported chain definitions, address validation rules, explorer URL patterns, supported locker sources, burn addresses of interest, and source priorities. This configuration may be stored in MongoDB or kept as static configuration loaded at startup.

For rule data, the internal model should be normalized. Ownership should capture whether the contract has an owner concept, what the owner address is, and whether ownership appears renounced based on chain-specific semantics. Liquidity should capture discovered pools, LP token distribution, whether LP tokens are in known lock contracts, held by burn addresses, or still in deployer-controlled wallets, and any lock expiry timestamps. Holder concentration should capture the top holders, excluded address categories such as burn or liquidity pool contracts where appropriate, and the resulting top-10 concentration percentage. The model must record exclusion logic explicitly so the response remains explainable.

Key flows:
1) Token scan flow
A user sends a token address or uses a scan command. The bot validates the input, determines the target chain, and checks whether a recent cached result exists. If the cache is fresh, the bot returns it immediately with a note that it is cached. Otherwise, the screening engine performs a live evaluation: fetch token metadata, resolve liquidity pools, inspect ownership state, inspect LP token custody for lock or burn evidence, obtain top-holder distribution, compute the top-10 concentration, apply rule thresholds, and generate a structured verdict. The Telegram layer formats the result into a short summary plus optional details.

2) Ownership renounced evaluation flow
The engine checks whether the token contract exposes ownership semantics and determines the current owner or equivalent admin authority. A token should only be marked as renounced if the owner has been set to a recognized null or burn-like address, or if the contract design clearly indicates no remaining owner authority. If the contract is non-standard or the owner model is unclear, the result should be unknown rather than falsely passing.

3) Liquidity locked or burned flow
The engine discovers the primary liquidity pool or pools using supported DEX and pair sources. It then inspects LP token supply and where LP tokens are held. If a sufficient share of LP tokens is held in recognized lock contracts with a future unlock time, the rule passes as locked. If LP tokens are irreversibly sent to recognized burn addresses, the rule passes as burned. If most LP remains under deployer or related wallet control, the rule fails. If pool discovery is incomplete, the rule becomes unknown with a source limitation message.

4) Top-10 holder concentration flow
The engine fetches holder distribution data, classifies addresses into categories such as burn, liquidity pool, contract, treasury, or externally owned wallet, and computes top-10 concentration according to the selected policy. The policy should define whether burn and LP addresses are excluded from concentration checks. The result passes if the aggregate top-10 share is at or below the threshold, for example 25%. The response should list the computed percentage and whether exclusions were applied.

5) Symbol or name lookup flow
If the user sends a symbol instead of an address, the bot should first ask for chain selection when ambiguity exists, then present candidate tokens or request a contract address for accuracy. Production design should treat symbols as convenience only, because they are ambiguous and vulnerable to impersonation. The bot should favor contract-address-based scans.

6) Group usage flow
In group chats, the bot should support inline-style simple scans triggered by replying with a token address or using a command. To reduce noise, group defaults can be concise. Detailed reports can be sent only on explicit request. Group administrators can configure default chain and thresholds.

7) Failure and fallback flow
If a source is unavailable or a chain is unsupported, the bot should return a partial result rather than a generic failure when possible. For example, it may report ownership and holder concentration but mark liquidity status as unknown. Every unknown should include the reason, such as unsupported locker source, missing pool discovery, or temporary upstream timeout.

8) Caching and deduplication flow
When multiple users request the same token within a short interval, the bot should avoid repeated live scans. An in-memory in-flight map can collapse concurrent requests into one active scan. Completed results are stored in MongoDB with freshness metadata and may also be kept in memory for very short-term reuse. This reduces load and improves response times.

Tech stack:
The stack should remain intentionally simple and aligned with the constraints.

1) Runtime and language
Use Node.js with ESM modules as the single application runtime.

2) Telegram framework
Use grammY for update handling, command routing, middleware, context management, and conversational interactions. The bot token should be read from the TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

3) Persistence
Use MongoDB for durable persistence of users, chats, scan history, cached scan results, watchlists, and configuration where persistence is useful. For optional ephemeral concerns such as rate-limit counters, in-flight scan deduplication, and very short-lived hot cache entries, use in-memory maps within the single process.

4) External data access
Use blockchain RPC endpoints and any necessary metadata or indexing APIs through isolated adapter modules. These are application integrations, not new infrastructure components. All such access should be abstracted behind interfaces so providers can be changed without affecting the Telegram or domain layers.

5) Storage for exports or artifacts
If the bot later supports downloadable reports or images, use object storage or simple filesystem storage. This is optional and should not be required for the initial release.

6) Deployment model
Run the application as a single Node.js server in the cloud. Keep startup simple, environment-driven, and compatible with webhook or polling operation. No additional databases, queues, or distributed coordination systems should be introduced.

7) Configuration and secrets
Use environment variables for Telegram token, MongoDB connection details, RPC endpoints, and feature flags. Keep chain-specific thresholds and rule defaults in configuration with optional MongoDB-backed overrides.

8) Observability
Use structured application logs, lightweight health checks, and internal metrics counters within the Node.js service. Avoid adding external infrastructure dependencies beyond what is already allowed.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability should center on graceful degradation. The bot should return partial results with explicit unknown states when upstream blockchain or indexing sources are unavailable. It should avoid silent failures and should record source errors in logs tied to a scan request ID for troubleshooting.

Performance targets should favor fast perceived responses. A cached response should be nearly immediate. A live scan should aim to complete within a few seconds under normal source conditions. To support this, the system should parallelize independent lookups, enforce per-source timeouts, and cap the amount of data fetched for holder analysis based on what is actually needed for the top-10 rule.

Security and abuse prevention are important even for a read-only bot. The bot should validate all user inputs, restrict the length and format of free text, and rate-limit scans per user and per chat. If symbol lookup is supported, the bot should clearly warn that symbols are ambiguous. Administrative commands in groups should be permission-gated. Secrets must remain in environment variables and never be exposed in error messages.

Data correctness should be framed carefully. The bot is evaluating heuristics, not certifying safety. Each rule needs a clearly documented definition and exclusion policy. For example, the holder concentration calculation must state whether liquidity pool and burn addresses are excluded. This avoids misleading users and reduces support burden.

Maintainability should come from modular design, typed internal contracts, centralized rule definitions, and adapter isolation. Adding a new chain or liquidity locker source should not require changing command logic. Each adapter should be independently testable with fixture-driven tests.

Operational simplicity matters because the service runs as one process. The design should avoid background workloads that can overwhelm the runtime. If watchlists or rescans are added, schedule them conservatively and ensure user-triggered scans remain prioritized. Cache freshness policies should be tuned so expensive live scans are not repeated unnecessarily while still keeping results useful.

Compliance and user trust should be supported through clear disclaimers in the bot copy. The bot should state that results are informational, may be incomplete, and are not financial advice. This is especially important because labels such as renounced or locked can be misunderstood as guarantees.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces the bot, explains what checks are supported, and tells the user how to submit a token for screening. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Shows supported commands, accepted token input formats, chain selection guidance, and explanation of pass, fail, and unknown results. (usage: /help) [general]
• /scan — Runs the default token safety screen against a contract address using the standard rule profile. (usage: /scan <chain> <tokenaddress>) [screening]
• /check — Alias for the main screening command for users who prefer a simpler action verb. (usage: /check <chain> <tokenaddress>) [screening]
• /details — Returns a more detailed breakdown of the latest or specified token scan, including evidence for each rule. (usage: /details <chain> <tokenaddress>) [screening]
• /holders — Shows the top-holder concentration summary and whether the token passes the configured top-10 threshold. (usage: /holders <chain> <tokenaddress>) [analysis]
• /liquidity — Shows discovered liquidity pools and whether liquidity appears locked, burned, or deployer-controlled. (usage: /liquidity <chain> <tokenaddress>) [analysis]
• /ownership — Shows the ownership status of the token contract and whether renounce criteria are satisfied. (usage: /ownership <chain> <tokenaddress>) [analysis]
• /setthreshold — Updates the user or chat threshold for concentration checks such as the maximum top-10 holder percentage. (usage: /setthreshold top10 <percentage>) [settings]
• /setchain — Sets the default chain for future scans so the user does not need to repeat it every time. (usage: /setchain <chain>) [settings]
• /settings — Shows the current user or chat defaults, including preferred chain, concentration threshold, and output mode. (usage: /settings) [settings]
• /watch — Adds a token to a personal watchlist for future rescans or alerting features. (usage: /watch <chain> <tokenaddress>) [watchlist]
• /unwatch — Removes a token from the watchlist. (usage: /unwatch <chain> <tokenaddress>) [watchlist]
• /watchlist — Displays the user's saved watchlist entries and the latest known status summary for each. (usage: /watchlist) [watchlist]

planSections.productGoals:
The primary goal is to let a Telegram user submit a token identifier and receive a structured safety screen in seconds. The result should clearly answer whether the token appears to satisfy the requested conditions: ownership renounced, liquidity locked or burned, and top-10 holder concentration below the configured threshold. The bot should also show the evidence used for each result so users understand why a token passed or failed.

A second goal is to make the evaluation configurable without overwhelming users. The default profile can check a standard set of rules, while advanced users can optionally select a chain, adjust thresholds such as top-10 concentration, or request a more detailed report. This keeps the first-time experience simple while preserving flexibility for serious users.

A third goal is operational trustworthiness. Production readiness here means stable command handling, clear error reporting when a token cannot be resolved, protection against spam and repeated costly lookups, and a persistence model that stores user preferences, recent scans, and optional watchlists. The bot should be explicit about limitations: liquidity lock status may depend on supported locker sources, ownership renounce semantics vary by contract design, and holder concentration can change rapidly.

A fourth goal is explainability. Rather than returning a single opaque score, the bot should report a checklist with pass, fail, or unknown states for each rule, plus supporting details such as owner address status, liquidity pool location, lock/burn evidence, and top-holder share percentages. This makes the bot more useful and defensible than a simplistic yes/no label.
